title: Charmed Life - Chapter 15 (Fragment 3)
text:
Janet sighed. “That’s what I mean. I knew you’d get it if I ordered you to. Do you mind not being so kind and obedient? It makes me nervous. Anyway—” She took up the book. “Can you see your face in it?”

“Almost nothing else,” said Cat.

“Funny. I can see my face,” said Janet. “Can I do it, too?”

“You’re more likely to get it to work than I am,” said Cat.

So they both circled the mirror, and they said the words in chorus. The door opened. Mary came in. Janet guiltily put the book behind her back.

“Yes, here he is,” Mary said. She stood aside to let a strange young man come into the room. “This is Will Suggins,” she said. “He’s Euphemia’s young man. He wants to talk to you, Eric.”

Will Suggins was tall and burly and rather handsome. His clothes looked as if he had brushed them carefully after working in a bakery all day. He was not friendly. “It was you turned Euphemia into a frog, was it?” he said to Cat.

“Yes,” said Cat. He dared say nothing else with Mary there.

“You’re rather small,” said Will Suggins. He seemed disappointed about it. “Anyway,” he said, “whatever size you are, I’m not having Euphemia turned into things. I take exception to it. Understand?”

“I’m very sorry,” said Cat. “I won’t do it again.”

“Too right you won’t!” said Will Suggins. “You got off too light over this, by what Mary tells me. I’m going to teach you a lesson you won’t forget in a hurry.”

“No you’re not!” said Janet. She marched up to Will Suggins and pushed Magic for Beginners threateningly towards him. “You’re three times his size, and he’s said he’s sorry. If you touch Cat, I shall—” She took the book out of Will Suggins’ chest in order to leaf hastily through it. “I shall induce complete immobility in the legs and trunk.”

“And very pretty I shall look, I’m sure!” Will Suggins said, much amused. “How are you going to do that without your witchcraft, may I ask? And if you did, I daresay I could get out of it fast enough. I’m a fair warlock myself. Though,” he said, turning to Mary, “you might have warned me he was this small.”

“Not so small where witchcraft and mischief are concerned,” said Mary. “Neither of them are. They’re a pair of real bad lots.”

“Well, I’ll do it by witchcraft then. I’m easy,” said Will Suggins. He searched in the pockets of his slightly floury jacket. “Ah!” he said, and fetched out what seemed to be a lump of dough. For a moment he shaped it vigorously in both powerful hands. Then he rolled it into a ball and threw it at Cat’s feet. It landed on the carpet with a soft plop. Cat looked at it in great apprehension, wondering what it was supposed to do.

“That’ll lie there,” said Will Suggins, “until three o’clock Sunday. Sunday’s a bad time to be at witchcraft, but it’s my free day. I shall be waiting for you then in Bedlam Field, in the form of a tiger. I make a good tiger. You can turn yourself into something as large as you like, or small and fast, if you prefer, and I’ll teach you that lesson whatever you are. But if you don’t come to Bedlam Field in the form of something, that lump of dough will start to work and you’ll be a frog yourself—for as long as I feel like keeping you that way. Right, Mary. I’m through now.”

vocabulary.definition: Anxiety or fear that something bad or unpleasant will happen.
vocabulary.example: Cat was worried about what the lump of dough might do.

vocabulary.definition: Large and strong; heavily built.
vocabulary.example: Will Suggins had a strong and muscular appearance.

vocabulary.definition: A length of something wound or arranged in a spiral or sequence of rings.
A solemn utterance intended to invoke a supernatural power to inflict harm or punishment on someone.
Sad or displeased because someone or something has failed to fulfil one's hopes or expectations.
vocabulary.example: Will Suggins appeared unhappy with Cat's small size.

vocabulary.definition: A case to which a rule does not apply; objection or protest.
vocabulary.example: Will Suggins objected strongly to Euphemia being turned into things.

vocabulary.definition: The visible shape or configuration of something.
vocabulary.example: Will Suggins said he would appear as a tiger.

vocabulary.definition: In a way that shows or expresses guilt.
vocabulary.example: Janet behaved in a guilty manner when Mary came in.

vocabulary.definition: The state of being unable to move.
vocabulary.example: Janet’s spell would make the legs and trunk completely immobile.

vocabulary.definition: The ability to resist a particular infection or toxin; exemption from an obligation or penalty.
To cause or bring about.
vocabulary.example: Janet threatened to cause immobility using the book.

vocabulary.definition: An experience that teaches a person how to do something or behave.
vocabulary.example: Will Suggins threatened to teach Cat a memorable lesson.

vocabulary.definition: To be positioned or to rest in a flat position.
vocabulary.example: The lump of dough would remain where it landed until Sunday.

vocabulary.definition: A compact mass of a substance, usually without a definite or regular shape.
vocabulary.example: Will Suggins took out a compact mass of dough from his pocket.

vocabulary.definition: To walk with a regular and measured tread, often in a determined way.
vocabulary.example: Janet confidently approached Will Suggins.

vocabulary.definition: Playful misbehaviour or troublemaking, especially in children.
vocabulary.example: Mary suggested that both Cat and Janet are troublesome regarding witchcraft.

vocabulary.definition: Easily agitated or alarmed; anxious or apprehensive.
vocabulary.example: Janet said that Cat's obedient behaviour made her nervous.

vocabulary.definition: Willing to comply with orders or requests; submissive to another's authority.
vocabulary.example: Janet asked Cat not to be so kind and obedient as it made her nervous.

vocabulary.definition: A form of words used as a magical charm or incantation.
In a way that expresses an intention to cause harm or danger.
vocabulary.example: Janet pushed the book towards Will Suggins in a threatening manner.

vocabulary.definition: To make a thorough or dramatic change in form, appearance, or character.
vocabulary.example: Cat was told he could change his form by magic.

vocabulary.definition: The main part of the body excluding the head and limbs.
vocabulary.example: The spell targeted movement of the legs and the main body.

vocabulary.definition: In a way that involves physical strength, effort, or energy; strenuously.
vocabulary.example: Will Suggins strongly kneaded the dough.

vocabulary.definition: A man reputed to practise witchcraft; a male witch.
vocabulary.example: Will Suggins described himself as a capable male witch.

vocabulary.definition: To inform someone in advance of a possible danger, problem, or other unpleasant situation.
vocabulary.example: Will Suggins said Mary should have told him about Cat’s size.

vocabulary.definition: Evil or morally wrong.
